assets.description: Solanezumab is a humanized IgG1 monoclonal antibody developed by Eli Lilly that targets the mid-domain of soluble monomeric amyloid-beta (Abeta) peptide, intended to clear circulating soluble Abeta and shift equilibrium away from amyloid deposition in the brain ("peripheral sink" hypothesis). It was advanced as a disease-modifying therapy for Alzheimer disease, progressing from mild-to-moderate dementia populations into mild dementia and, ultimately, preclinical (asymptomatic, amyloid-positive) Alzheimer disease. At the point of decision it represented one of the most extensively studied anti-amyloid immunotherapies, with a favorable safety profile relative to plaque-targeting antibodies but persistent uncertainty about whether targeting soluble Abeta monomer could translate into clinical benefit.
assets.outcome_summary: Failure. Solanezumab failed to meet its primary cognitive endpoint in the Phase 3 EXPEDITION3 trial in mild Alzheimer dementia (reported at CTAD 2016), and Lilly declined to file for regulatory approval. A subsequent Phase 3 trial in preclinical/asymptomatic Alzheimer disease, the A4 study (reported 2023), also failed, showing no slowing of cognitive decline and no reduction in brain amyloid burden. Lilly ended clinical development of solanezumab; it was never approved or marketed.

sources.title: Lilly Announces Detailed Results of Solanezumab Phase 3 EXPEDITION3 Study at CTAD 2016
sources.note: Primary-source Lilly press release: EXPEDITION3 did not meet its primary endpoint in mild AD dementia; Lilly did not pursue regulatory submission.

sources.title: Solanezumab Fails Phase 3 A4 Study of Preclinical Alzheimer Disease (NeurologyLive)
sources.note: Reports the 2023 A4 Phase 3 failure in preclinical AD: no slowing of decline, no effect on amyloid burden.

sources.title: Solanezumab therapeutic profile (ALZFORUM)
sources.note: Comprehensive independent dossier of the full solanezumab trial program, mechanism, and outcomes.

evidence.text: Solanezumab binds the mid-region (epitope centered on residues ~16-24) of soluble monomeric amyloid-beta, with low affinity for fibrillar/deposited plaque. This selectivity was the central design premise: capture circulating soluble Abeta to drive a peripheral sink, distinguishing it mechanistically from plaque-binding antibodies.

Preclinical and biomarker work established target engagement: dosing produced large increases in total (antibody-bound) plasma and CSF Abeta, confirming the antibody engages soluble Abeta in vivo. However, whether lowering soluble monomer would alter the disease-driving aggregated/oligomeric species remained a live mechanistic uncertainty going in.

Solanezumab carried a comparatively benign safety profile, with notably low rates of amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA-E/edema and microhemorrhage) versus plaque-targeting antibodies, consistent with its soluble-monomer selectivity and lack of vascular plaque engagement. Tolerability was a relative strength of the asset.

As a humanized IgG1 monoclonal, immunogenicity risk was expected to be low and was not a program-defining liability; anti-drug antibody signals were not a prominent concern relative to the efficacy question.

Pharmacokinetics were consistent with a typical IgG monoclonal (long half-life supporting intermittent IV dosing), and the marked rise in bound plasma Abeta served as a readily measurable PD/target-engagement marker enabling dose selection into late-phase trials.

The amyloid hypothesis faced a heavily skeptical field after a string of anti-amyloid failures, and the soluble-monomer mechanism was contested. Strategically, Lilly's move into earlier (mild, then preclinical/asymptomatic) populations reflected the thesis that intervention timing, not mechanism, explained prior disappointments, a bet with meaningful clinical and commercial risk.

assets.caveats: Outcome is unambiguous: solanezumab was never approved and its development was terminated after two Phase 3 failures (EXPEDITION3, 2016, mild dementia; A4, 2023, preclinical AD). Nuance worth flagging for calibration: earlier EXPEDITION1/2 secondary/pooled analyses generated a debated signal in mild patients that justified continued development, so the program's mid-life read was more ambiguous than the final result. Evidence snippets are reconstructed editorial summaries of publicly known mechanism/biomarker/safety characteristics and deliberately contain no post-decision efficacy readouts or fabricated trial numbers.

assets.description: Gantenerumab is a fully human anti-amyloid-beta IgG1 monoclonal antibody developed by Roche (originating from MorphoSys HuCAL technology) that binds aggregated/fibrillar amyloid-beta to promote plaque clearance via microglial phagocytosis. It is formulated for subcutaneous administration, differentiating it from intravenous competitors in the anti-amyloid class. The asset entered a large pivotal Phase 3 program (GRADUATE I and II) in early symptomatic Alzheimer disease (mild cognitive impairment and mild AD dementia), using a high-dose, up-titrated regimen intended to overcome underdosing seen in earlier studies, with additional secondary-prevention and open-label extension trials running in parallel. The evaluation targets slowing of clinical decline on the CDR-SB global cognitive/functional endpoint.
assets.outcome_summary: Failure. On 14 November 2022 Roche reported that both Phase 3 GRADUATE I and GRADUATE II studies missed their primary endpoint: gantenerumab did not significantly slow clinical decline on CDR-SB versus placebo (relative reductions of ~8% and ~6%, not statistically significant). Amyloid removal was markedly lower than predicted (~25-28% of patients reaching amyloid-negativity at two years, roughly half the modeled level). Roche discontinued the gantenerumab program, including GRADUATION, OpenRoAD, PostGraduate, and SKYLINE. Later DIAN-TU secondary-prevention exploratory signals did not reverse the decision; the drug was never approved or marketed.

evidence.text: Gantenerumab is a fully human IgG1 engineered to bind aggregated/fibrillar amyloid-beta (plaque conformations) with subnanomolar affinity, engaging both N-terminal and central epitopes to recruit microglial Fc-mediated phagocytosis. Target-engagement rationale was well established pre-decision.

Prior imaging studies (SCarlet RoAD / Marguerite RoAD and open-label extensions) demonstrated dose-dependent reduction of amyloid plaque burden on PET, supporting on-target pharmacology and motivating the higher-dose GRADUATE regimen. However, the magnitude and consistency of clearance across patients was a known uncertainty going in.

Subcutaneous fortnightly dosing offered a convenience/access advantage over IV anti-amyloids, but earlier trials showed the initial dose was subtherapeutic, forcing an up-titration strategy; whether SC exposure would deliver sufficient CNS antibody to hit modeled clearance targets was an open ADME/PK risk pre-readout.

Amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA-E edema and ARIA-H microhemorrhage) were a recognized dose-dependent class liability for aggregated-amyloid antibodies, requiring MRI monitoring and titration; a known safety headwind that constrains dosing latitude.
As a fully human antibody, gantenerumab carried comparatively low anti-drug-antibody risk relative to chimeric/humanized constructs, a favorable but not decisive design feature.

Roche/MorphoSys asset entering a crowded, high-risk anti-amyloid landscape shaped by aducanumab's contested 2021 approval and emerging lecanemab data; enormous unmet need but a validation-thin, controversial mechanism where several predecessors had failed pivotal trials.

assets.caveats: Outcome is unambiguous as a development failure (missed both pivotal endpoints, program discontinued, never approved). One nuance worth flagging: gantenerumab was continued in the separate DIAN-TU platform for autosomal-dominant/early-onset genetic AD, where later exploratory analyses suggested a possible delay in symptom onset in a small pre-symptomatic subgroup. This does not change the sporadic-AD commercial failure verdict but means the molecule's scientific story is not purely negative. Effect-size and amyloid-clearance figures cited are from Roche/editorial disclosures, not reconstructed.

assets.description: Evacetrapib (LY2484595) is an orally administered small-molecule cholesteryl ester transfer protein (CETP) inhibitor developed by Eli Lilly for high-risk atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and dyslipidemia. By blocking CETP, it was designed to markedly raise HDL cholesterol while lowering LDL cholesterol, on the hypothesis that this favorable lipid shift would translate into reduced major adverse cardiovascular events. The program advanced to a large Phase 3 cardiovascular outcomes trial (ACCELERATE) in patients with established high-risk atherosclerotic disease. The pre-decision question was whether its striking lipid effects would deliver a clinical outcomes benefit in a drug class with a troubled prior history.
assets.outcome_summary: Failure. On October 12, 2015, Eli Lilly accepted the independent data monitoring committee's recommendation to terminate the Phase 3 ACCELERATE outcomes trial (~12,000 high-risk patients) for futility, citing low probability of hitting the primary cardiovascular endpoint. The drug produced large lipid changes (roughly +130% HDL, -37% LDL) but no meaningful reduction in cardiovascular events versus placebo. Lilly discontinued development; the drug was never approved or marketed. The stoppage was for lack of efficacy, not safety.

sources.note: Primary source: Lilly's official announcement of program discontinuation after ACCELERATE termination.

sources.title: Lilly Provides Update on Evacetrapib Phase 3 Trial
sources.note: Lilly investor release describing the Oct 12, 2015 IDMC recommendation to stop ACCELERATE for insufficient efficacy (not safety).

sources.title: ACCELERATE Shows Third Time's No Charm for CETP Inhibitors (Cleveland Clinic)

evidence.text: Evacetrapib is a potent, selective small-molecule inhibitor of CETP, a validated target for HDL modulation; on-target pharmacology was well characterized and mechanism-of-action was not in doubt entering pivotal testing.

The core scientific rationale rested on the lipid hypothesis: raising HDL and lowering LDL should reduce atherosclerotic events. Robust biomarker effects were demonstrable, but the causal link between CETP-driven lipid changes and hard cardiovascular outcomes remained unproven pharmacology.
Earlier-phase work established oral bioavailability and a dose producing large, sustained changes in HDL and LDL, supporting once-daily dosing and progression into a large outcomes trial.

Unlike the earlier CETP inhibitor torcetrapib, evacetrapib showed no off-target blood-pressure or aldosterone signal, and the trial was ultimately not halted for safety, indicating a cleaner tolerability profile within the class.

The CETP-inhibitor class carried a heavy prior-failure burden at the time of decision: torcetrapib (Pfizer) failed on excess mortality and dalcetrapib (Roche) failed for lack of efficacy, raising substantial class-level risk that favorable lipid biomarkers would not translate to outcomes.

Prior class experience suggested that CETP-mediated HDL increases may yield dysfunctional or less protective HDL particles, undermining confidence that the biomarker improvements would confer clinical cardiovascular benefit.

assets.caveats: Outcome is unambiguous and well-documented via Lilly's own press releases and independent clinical coverage: ACCELERATE was stopped for futility and development discontinued; never approved. Nuance worth noting for calibration: the failure was efficacy/futility, not safety, and the drug demonstrated strong on-target biomarker effects, so this is a "target/mechanism did not translate to outcomes" failure rather than a toxicity or PK failure. The reconstructed evidence profile avoids leaking the terminal result; specific trial efficacy numbers are stated only in the post-outcome summary, not the pre-decision evidence snippets. No fabricated preclinical or trial figures were introduced.
assets.name: Evacetrapib

assets.description: Tarenflurbil (Flurizan, MPC-7869) is the R-enantiomer of the NSAID flurbiprofen, developed by Myriad Genetics/Myriad Pharmaceuticals (with partner Lundbeck) as an orally administered selective amyloid-beta-42-lowering agent (SALA) acting as a gamma-secretase modulator. Unlike NSAID-mechanism drugs, the R-enantiomer lacks COX inhibition, aiming to shift gamma-secretase cleavage of APP toward shorter, less amyloidogenic Abeta species without anti-inflammatory GI toxicity. The candidate targeted mild Alzheimer disease and advanced into large 18-month Phase 3 pivotal testing in North America.
assets.outcome_summary: Failure. On June 30, 2008, Myriad announced tarenflurbil did not meet either co-primary endpoint (cognition and function/activities of daily living) versus placebo in its 1,684-patient, 18-month North American Phase 3 trial in mild Alzheimer disease. Myriad and Lundbeck discontinued the program, including the ongoing international Phase 3. The failure is widely attributed to weak pharmacodynamics: poor blood-brain barrier penetration and low target potency, so brain exposure was insufficient to engage gamma-secretase at effective concentrations. The drug was never approved or marketed.

evidence.text: Mechanistic hypothesis was that R-flurbiprofen acts as a gamma-secretase modulator, allosterically shifting APP cleavage toward shorter Abeta species and selectively lowering Abeta42 without inhibiting overall proteolysis or Notch processing. Mechanism was plausible and differentiated (a SALA), but modulator potency at gamma-secretase was known to be modest.

A recognized liability entering pivotal testing was CNS exposure: the compound's ability to cross the blood-brain barrier and reach brain concentrations adequate for target engagement was uncertain, given the doses required relative to achievable brain levels.

Derivation from R-flurbiprofen, an enantiomer lacking meaningful COX inhibition, was expected to avoid the GI and platelet toxicity of classical NSAIDs, supporting tolerability for chronic dosing in an elderly AD population.

Preclinical models and mechanistic studies supported selective Abeta42 lowering as an anti-amyloid strategy, and a preceding smaller Phase 2 study generated enough signal in milder patients to justify advancing to Phase 3, though the earlier signal was subgroup-driven and not robust.

As a repurposed single enantiomer of an off-patent NSAID advanced by Myriad with commercialization partner Lundbeck, the program carried a first-in-class SALA positioning but faced composition-of-matter and differentiation questions typical of enantiomer/repurposing assets.

assets.caveats: Outcome is unambiguous: the pivotal Phase 3 missed both co-primary endpoints and the program was terminated in 2008; never approved. Evidence snippets are reconstructed editorial summaries of the pre-2008 knowledge state (mechanism, expected CNS-penetration risk, NSAID-derived tolerability rationale) and deliberately contain no trial efficacy numbers or outcome leakage. The BBB-penetration/target-engagement framing is the widely accepted post-hoc explanation; the degree to which it was quantified before the readout is somewhat retrospective.

Myriad and partner Lundbeck discontinued the program including the ongoing international Phase 3. Never approved or marketed. The failure is widely attributed to weak pharmacodynamics — inadequate blood-brain-barrier penetration / insufficient gamma-secretase target engagement at achievable brain concentrations. All numbers (1,684, 18 months, date) and the mechanistic post-mortem match the summary exactly.

(b) Sources support it. All four cited sources are real, on-topic, and corroborate: Wikipedia (Tarenflurbil), ALZFORUM Flurizan therapeutics entry, PMC5350742 (peer-reviewed "Translational Medicine Lessons from Flurizan's Failure" attributing failure to inadequate brain penetration/target engagement), and the ALZFORUM Phase 3 data paper-alert. One mild caveat: the evidence is somewhat hindsight-tinged — E2 (adverse CNS exposure) and E1 (modest modulator potency) foreground exactly the two liabilities later blamed for the failure, and E4 downgrades the Phase 2 signal as "subgroup-driven and not robust." This telegraphs a pessimistic prior but is legitimately knowable pre-Phase-3 and stops short of revealing the outcome.
